| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Shape | Prismatic (thickness ( 0.1 mm )/width (mm)/height (mm) |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| Height, mm | 48 | 48 | 48 | 48 | 67.3 | 50 | 78 | 151 | 128 | 208 |
| Width, mm | 19.5 | 30 | 30 | 30.5 | 35.1 | 34.1 | 61 | 95 | 89 | 130 |
| Thickness, mm | 6.1 | 4.6 | 5.5 | 8.1 | 6.5 | 10.3 | 16 | 28 | 46 | 50 |
| Volume, ml. | 5.70 | 6.6 | 7.9 | 11.9 | 15.3 | 18.4 | 76 | 136 | 465 | 1352 |

| Specific energy (Wh/kg) | 135 | 137 | 138 | 139 | 104 | 146 | 145 | 145 | 156 | 160 |
| Energy density (Wh/L) | 272 | 291 | 281 | 280 | 226 | 301 | 345 | 344 | 372 | 430 |


The most significant challenges to the broader application of Li-ion technologies are related to either stability at high temperature or safety. While batteries may be exposed to temperatures as high as $70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for short periods, the rate of degradation of current Li -ion batteries is significant above $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Li-ion batteries are generally safe, although venting can occur if they are overcharged or crushed. To vent from overcharge, batteries must typically be charged to greater than $200 \%$ of their rated capacity. Protective devices are employed to prevent ventings under abusive conditions.

In current Li-ion batteries, the overcharge, overdischarge and over temperature issues have been largely addressed by the incorporation of management circuits into batteries to provide protection from over-charge, over-discharge or over-temperature. In addition, the circuits may also perform fuel gauge functions and can record battery history. The controller in the management circuit typically monitors the voltage of each cell or string of cells in a battery. In addition, circuits typically include a thermistor or other thermostat for restorable overtemperature control and a thermal fuse for non-restorable over-temperature control. Additional information on battery management circuits is found in Sec. 5.6.

### 35.4.2 Performance of Commerical Cylindrical Batteries

Discharge Rate Capability. The rate capability and capacity of Li-ion batteries are dependent on their design, and varies considerably between manufacturers. Discharge curves for 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries discharged at rates from 0.33 A to 3.6 A at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are shown in Fig. 35.36. At low rates ( $\mathrm{C} / 2$ ), the battery provided 1.67 Ah , and at 1.65 A (the 1 C rate) 1.60 Ah while at the C rate the average voltage was 3.5 V .

Batteries utilizing the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ cell chemistry have also been commercialized. The spinel cathode materials, as described in the section on cathode materials, are regarded as providing lower cost and more benign safety properties relative to $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$, although they possess lower specific capacity. Shown in Fig. 35.37 is the rate capability and capacity of 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ batteries. The products provide 1.44 Ah at the $\mathrm{C} / 5$ rate ( 0.28 A ) and 1.34 Ah at the C rate ( 1.4 A ), comparable to, but less than the capacity of $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries. The discharge curves have a flat voltage profile with average voltage at the C rate of $3.65 \mathrm{~V}, 0.15 \mathrm{~V}$ higher than $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ cells at the C rate.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1091.jpg?height=542&width=1076&top_left_y=1410&top_left_x=389)
FIGURE 35.36 Discharge capability of 18650 type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries at constant current at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of the University of South Carolina and NEC Moli Energy.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1092.jpg?height=593&width=1106&top_left_y=181&top_left_x=383)
FIGURE 35.37 Rate capability of an 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ battery at constant current. The battery was charged in a CCCV (constant current-constant voltage) regime at 1.4 A to 4.2 V for 2.5 hours, then discharged at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

The performance of Li -ion batteries under constant power discharge is illustrated in Fig. 35.38 for 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries, and in Fig. 35.39 for an 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ battery. At 1 W the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery provided 6.3 Wh , or $145 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $380 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$. At 1 W the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ battery provided 5.5 Wh , or $130 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $332 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$.

The rate capability of several 18650-type batteries is compared in Fig. 35.40 which illustrates the higher capacity of newer products and the 3C rate capability of the A\&T LSR18650 product. The average discharge voltage is lowered significantly at the higher rates.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1092.jpg?height=614&width=1242&top_left_y=1338&top_left_x=314)
FIGURE 35.38 Constant power discharge of an 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery. The battery was charged in a CCCV regime at 1.65 A to 4.2 V for 2.5 hours at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1093.jpg?height=612&width=1292&top_left_y=181&top_left_x=279)
FIGURE 35.39 Constant power discharge of a $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} 18650$ battery. The battery was charged in a CCCV regime at 1.4 A to 4.2 V for 2.5 hours at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1093.jpg?height=649&width=1290&top_left_y=924&top_left_x=281)
FIGURE 35.40 Comparison of the rate capability at constant current for $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2} 18650$ batteries.

High and Low Temperature Discharge. The voltage and capacity on discharge at the $\mathrm{C} / 5$ rate for 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries at high and low temperatures are illustrated in Fig. 35.41, for a $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ battery, and in Fig. 35.42 for a $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ product. Comparing the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries, the spinel product provides nominally 0.25 V higher voltage, although the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ product provides typically $20 \%$ more coulombic capacity at any given temperature than the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ unit. Between $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4} 18650$ battery delivered 1.4 Ah with an average voltage of 3.9 V above $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and 3.7 V at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. At $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ product provided 1.3 Ah at 3.5 V , on average.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1094.jpg?height=655&width=1086&top_left_y=179&top_left_x=394)
FIGURE 35.41 Discharge of an 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ battery at various temperatures when charged in a CCCV (constant current-constant voltage) regime at 1.4 A to 4.2 V for 2.5 hours and discharged at $0.28 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{C} / 5)$. The average voltage at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was 3.9 V , at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 3.5$ V. (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1094.jpg?height=597&width=1138&top_left_y=1024&top_left_x=366)
FIGURE 35.42 Discharge at 0.2 C ( 0.33 A ) of an 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery at various temperatures when charged in a CCCV regime at 1.65 A to 4.2 V for 2.5 hours and discharged at 0.33 A (C/5 rate). The average voltage at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was 3.6 V , at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, 3.2 V. (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

As illustrated by Fig. 35.42, $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries provided similar capacities over the range of temperatures evaluated. At or above $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the battery provided 1.6 Ah and 1.5 Ah at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The average voltage at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was 3.6 V , and at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 3.2 \mathrm{~V}$.

At high rates, self-heating effects are observed upon discharge at low temperature. This is illustrated in Fig. 35.43 which shows discharge curves at 1.5 A ( 0.9 C rate) for an 18650type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ battery at temperatures from $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. At $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or above, the discharge curves appear similar to those in Fig. 35.42 for the $\mathrm{C} / 5$ rate as the battery delivered similar capacity ( 1.64 Ah ) with similar average voltage ( 3.6 V ). At $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the voltage recovered in mid-discharge as self-heating ensued. At $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, 1.2 Ah was delivered at 3.0 V on average. At $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1.0 \mathrm{Ah}$ was delivered at 2.8 V , on average.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1095.jpg?height=666&width=1260&top_left_y=186&top_left_x=296)
FIGURE 35.43 Approximate C-rate discharge of an 18650-type C/LiCoO2 battery at various temperatures when charged in a CCCV regime at 1.65 A to 4.2 V for 2.5 hours, then discharged at 1.5 A . The average voltage at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was 3.6 V , and at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 2.9 \mathrm{~V}$. (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

The capacity and energy of 18650 -type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries when discharged at 0.2 C rate are summarized in Fig. 35.44. Illustrated in the figure is the higher capacity and energy available from the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ chemistry, although because of the higher voltage of the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ product, the energy difference is less than the capacity difference at any given temperature. As shown, the batteries offer consistent capacity and energy between $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $90 \%$ of the $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ capacity at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The average cell voltage is depressed significantly at the lower temperatures.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1095.jpg?height=653&width=944&top_left_y=1342&top_left_x=454)
FIGURE 35.44 Discharge capacity and energy of 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ batteries at $0.2 \mathrm{C}(0.28 \mathrm{~A})$, and $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries at 0.2 C rate ( 0.33 A ), at temperatures from $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to 2.5 V . (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

Cycle Life of Commercial Products. A desirable characteristic of Li-ion batteries is their long cycle life and ability to cycle continuously at ambient, low, or elevated temperature. This is illustrated in Fig. 35.45 for a $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery which shows the discharge capacity when cycled at either $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at either the $1 \mathrm{C}, 0.5 \mathrm{C}$ or 0.2 C rate. As shown, the fade rate in all cases was comparable and relatively linear. At $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the batteries had a fade rate of $0.024 \% /$ cycle, while the battery at $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ had a fade rate of $0.028 \% /$ cycle. Thus after 500 cycles, the battery cycled at the 1 C rate at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ delivered $1.42 \mathrm{Ah}, 91 \%$ of the initial value, and the one cycled at $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at the 1 C rate delivered 1.35 Ah on the $500^{\text {th }}$ cycle, or $87 \%$ of the initial value.

The capacity of 18650 -type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ batteries cycled at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at the $1 \mathrm{C}, 0.5 \mathrm{C}$ or 0.2 C rates is illustrated in Fig. 35.46 . At $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at the 1 C rate, after 75 cycles, the fade rate was linear at $0.42 \% /$ cycle, and at the 0.5 C rate, $0.32 \% /$ cycle. After 500 cycles at the 1C rate, the battery delivered 1.01 Ah , or $70 \%$ of the initial capacity. For comparison, the cell cycled at the 0.5 C rate at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ delivered 1.16 Ah after 500 cycles, or $80 \%$ of its initial capacity. Higher fade rates are observed at $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

As batteries are cycled their impedance increases, resulting in reduced voltage and lower delivered energy. Illustrated in Fig. 35.47 are discharge curves at various cycles for a $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery cycled at the 1 C rate at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. As shown, the battery voltage decreased by typically $0.3 \mathrm{mV} /$ cycle. For comparison, Fig. 35.48 shows discharge curves at various cycles for an 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ battery cycled at the 1 C rate at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. As the cycling proceeded, the average voltage decreased, by $0.3 \mathrm{mV} /$ cycle, comparable to that observed with the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ chemistry.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1096.jpg?height=733&width=950&top_left_y=1007&top_left_x=461)
FIGURE 35.45 Capacity of 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries when cycled at either $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at rates from $0.2 \mathrm{C}(0.33 \mathrm{~A})$ to $1.0 \mathrm{C}(1.65 \mathrm{~A})$ between 4.2 V and 2.5 V. (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1097.jpg?height=694&width=902&top_left_y=184&top_left_x=474)
FIGURE 35.46 Capacity fade of 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ batteries when cycled at various rates at either $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ between 4.2 V and 2.5 V . (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1097.jpg?height=568&width=880&top_left_y=1271&top_left_x=485)
FIGURE 35.47 Discharge curves for an 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery when cycled at $1.65 \mathrm{~A}(1 \mathrm{C})$ charge and discharge illustrating the increase in resistance upon cycling. (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1098.jpg?height=636&width=1032&top_left_y=164&top_left_x=418)
FIGURE 35.48 Discharge curves from an 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ battery at various cycle numbers illustrating the increase in resistance upon cycling. The battery was charged in a CCCV regime at 1.4 A to 4.2 V for 2.5 hours, and discharged at 1.35 A . (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

Storage Performance. Two criteria that can be used to characterize the storage performance of batteries are their self-discharge rate and capacity recovery after storage. The capacity of a battery may be determined by discharge before storage. After storage in the charged state, discharge enables determination of self-discharge, subsequent charge and discharge enables determination of capacity recovery, also termed reversible capacity. These effects are illustrated by the discharge curves in Fig. 35.49. As shown, the voltage is minimally reduced ( 0.1 V ) by extended storage ( 6 months) at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and while self-discharge of $10 \%$ occurred over six months, after the battery was charged, $97 \%$ of the original capacity was delivered. For a 17500 -type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ Li-ion battery, the self-discharge performance (capacity retention) is illustrated in Fig. 35.50 and capacity recovery further illustrated in Fig. 35.51. Selfdischarge for 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries over a range of temperatures is illustrated in Fig. 35.52. As shown, at low and ambient temperatures, low self-discharge was observed, $11.5 \%$ over six months at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $25 \%$ at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. However, over 6 months at $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 80 \%$ self-discharge occurred.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1098.jpg?height=527&width=967&top_left_y=1503&top_left_x=452)
FIGURE 35.49 Discharge of a 17500-type cylindrical $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery before and after six months storage at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in a fully discharged state. Charge conditions: Constant voltage/constant current, $4.2 \mathrm{~V}, 550 \mathrm{~mA}$ (max.), 2 hours, $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Discharge conditions: Constant current 156 mA to 3.0 V at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Panasonic.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1099.jpg?height=649&width=1147&top_left_y=181&top_left_x=355)
FIGURE 35.50 Self-discharge performance of a 17500-type cylindrical $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ when discharged after storage in the charged state for periods of up to six months. Charge conditions: Constant voltage/constant current, $4.2 \mathrm{~V}, 550 \mathrm{~mA}$ (max.), 2 hours, $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Discharge conditions: Constant current 156 mA to 3.0 V at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Panasonic.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1099.jpg?height=620&width=1123&top_left_y=1167&top_left_x=366)
FIGURE 35.51 Capacity of a 17500-type cylindrical $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery when charged then discharged after storage at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in the fully charged state for periods of up to six months. Charge conditions: Constant voltage/constant current, $4.2 \mathrm{~V}, 550 \mathrm{~mA}$ (max.), 2 hours, $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Discharge conditions: Constant current 156 mA to 3.0 V at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Panasonic.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1100.jpg?height=532&width=1006&top_left_y=181&top_left_x=435)
FIGURE 35.52 Self-discharge performance of fully charged $18650 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2} \mathrm{Li}$-ion batteries over a range of temperatures. (Courtesy of Sanyo.)

### 35.4.3 Large Cylindrical Batteries

To power space probes, such as those intended for use by NASA to explore Mars, 25 Ah cylindrical $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2} \mathrm{Li}$-ion batteries have been developed. Given the mission's low temperature requirements, these batteries have been designed to charge and discharge at low temperature. An electrolyte of $1 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{LiPF}_{6}$ in EC:EMC is used. The capacity of a 25 Ah cylindrical cell when cycled at $23^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at 5 A , on charge and discharge, is shown in Fig. 35.53. The cell delivered 26.8 Ah initially and 24.1 Ah on the $500^{\text {th }}$ cycle, thus provides a fade rate of $0.02 \% /$ cycle. Figure 35.54 shows the capacity of another 25 Ah cell when cycled at the $\mathrm{C} / 5$ rate (charge and discharge) at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This 890 g cell, with a nominal capacity of 29 Ah at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ( $121 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $280 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$ ), delivered comparable capacity at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and over 25 Ah at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1100.jpg?height=556&width=709&top_left_y=1342&top_left_x=582)
FIGURE 35.53 Capacity of a 25 Ah cylindrical Li-ion battery when cycled at the $\mathrm{C} / 5$ rate between 4.1 V with taper charge to $\mathrm{C} / 50$ and 3.0 V for 500 cycles at $23^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Blue Star Advanced Technologies. Reproduced with permission from SAE paper 01-1390, 1999.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1101.jpg?height=710&width=1164&top_left_y=181&top_left_x=342)
FIGURE 35.54 Capacity of a 25 Ah cylindrical Li -ion cells when cycled at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at the C/5 rate between 4.1 and 3.0 V. (Courtesy of Blue Star Advanced Technologies. Reproduced with permission from SAE paper 01-1390, 1999.)

40 Ah Cylindrical Batteries for Low Earth Orbit Satellites. A unique battery application is in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites. This application is more completely described in the chapter on Nickel Hydrogen batteries (Chap. 32). Satellites in low earth orbit (550 km) orbit the earth approximately every 100 minutes, or approximately 15 times per day. When in sunlight, the batteries are charged, while during eclipse they are discharged. Over the course of the year, depending on the specific orbit, the eclipse duration varies, typically from 26 to 36 minutes. Given this regime in which the discharge rate is nominally twice the charge rate, LEO batteries are typically required to provide 30,000 to 50,000 cycles enabling a 5 - to 10 -year operational life. LEO applications are currently addressed almost entirely using nickel-hydrogen batteries as they offer high cycle life, despite their low specific energy ( 64 $\mathrm{Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ ) and low energy density ( $105 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$ ). Li-ion cells are being developed for LEO applications as their use will enable the development of dramatically lighter, smaller and less expensive satellite batteries, if the required cycle life can be obtained. Because of the high energy efficiency of Li-ion cells ( 95 to $98 \%$ ) on discharge and charge relative to $\mathrm{Ni}-\mathrm{H}_{2}$ cells ( 80 to $85 \%$ ), further reduction in spacecraft weight and cost is anticipated as improved energy efficiency permits a reduction of other systems, including solar arrays, the electrical buss and thermal controls.

Described here is the performance of 40 Ah cylindrical Li-ion cells tested over 1.33 years under LEO requirements. The cells provided over 40 Ah ( 41 Ah to 43 Ah ) or $120 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ when charged to 4.0 V and discharged at the $\mathrm{C} / 3$ rate to 2.7 V . The case negative cells utilized a stainless steel case and glass to metal seals. The cell chemistry was based on a $\mathrm{LiNiO}_{2}$ positive electrode, coated onto Al with a PVDF binder, and graphite negative coated onto copper. The separator was Celanese 2300, a microporous tri-layer laminated PPE/PE/ PPE film. ${ }^{90}$ The $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiNiO}_{2}$ chemistry is not used in commercial cells due to safety issues.

Three accelerated LEO regimes were used to shorten the typical 100 minute orbit time. One set utilized a 24 minute orbit and cycled at $20 \%$ DOD ( 8 Ah ), a second set utilized a 44 minute orbit and cycled at $20 \%$ DOD ( 8 Ah ), the third set cycled within a 47 minute orbit and cycled at $30 \%$ DOD ( 12 Ah ). The test parameters are summarized in Table 35.14. In this regime, cells cycling at $20 \%$ DOD occurred between $60 \%$ and $40 \%$ state of charge (SOC), and those at $30 \%$ DOD cycled between $60 \%$ and $30 \%$ SOC. The accelerated regime was used for 48 of every 50 cycles, the remaining two cycles were performed at normal LEO rates to determine the residual capacity. In this testing, the cells were operated at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with an end of charge (EOC) voltage of 3.75 V .

The residual energy of the cells (the discharge energy to $100 \%$ DOD at $\mathrm{C} / 3$ rate), with the recommended end of charge voltage of 3.75 V , is shown in Fig. 35.55. The data presented in this figure are the average of three cells. As shown, the energy fade rate for the longest regime ( $20 \%$ DOD, 44 minute) is one-third less than that of the faster regimes. In the worst case, the cells cycled at $30 \%$ DOD had an average fade rate of $0.00092 \% /$ cycle.

Using this data, the cycle life of the batteries in a LEO application has been estimated. Here, the cycle life was defined as that when residual energy upon discharge from 3.75 V to 2.7 V was 42 Wh for cells cycled at $30 \%$ DOD or 28 Wh for cells cycled to $20 \%$ DOD. The results of a calculation based on the energy fade data, considered to be the most conservative, are presented in Table 35.15. As shown, cycle lives between 55,000 and 137,000 cycles are predicted, suggesting lithium ion technology is capable of meeting LEO satellite requirements. These projections are based upon cycling these batteries in the middle of the useable voltage range.

TABLE 35.14 Parameters for the Accelerated LEO Testing
|  | Discharge rate | Discharge time | Charge rate | Charge time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 20\% DOD | 60 A | 8 min . | 35 A | 16 min . |
| 20\% DOD | 60 A | 8 min . | 15 A | 36 min . |
| 30\% DOD | 60 A | 12 min . | 25 A | 35 min . |


![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1102.jpg?height=616&width=787&top_left_y=1371&top_left_x=541)
FIGURE 35.55 The residual energy of a 40 Ah Li-ion cell cycled in an accelerated LEO regime. (Courtesy of SAFT.)

TABLE 35.15 Summary of LEO Test Results and Predicted Cycle Life. (Courtesy of SAFT.)
| Test regime | Cycles completed | Predicted cycle life |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $20 \%$ DOD, 24 minute | 21,000 | 91,833 |
| $20 \%$ OD, 44 minute | 12,500 | 137,663 |
| $30 \%$ DOD, 47 minute | 12,000 | 55,071 |


### 35.4.4 Prismatic Li-ion Batteries

Prismatic Li-ion batteries also offer a high level of performance, although design considerations related to the geometry result in differences in performance. For example, current cylindrical 18650 cells manufactured by Sanyo provide 1.7 Ah and weigh 42 g , thus offer $150 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $380 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$. A comparably sized prismatic battery, such as the UF103450P product by Sanyo, provides 1.5 Ah and weighs 38 g , thus offers similar specific energy ( 146 $\mathrm{Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ ) but lower energy density ( $301 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$ ). Some of this difference may result from unused space remaining in the cell case, such as that resulting from the insertion of a wound electrode stack into a prismatic can, while other differences occur because in cylindrical designs the cell case may be loaded with hoop stress without deformation, whereas if pressure is applied to the broad face of a prismatic design, bulging will result.

These factors change when comparing flat-plate to wound prismatic cell designs. As described below, many of the larger batteries utilize a flat-plate design whereas small cells almost universally utilize a wound design consistent with commercial cost requirements.

When $\mathrm{LiNi}_{x} \mathrm{Co}_{1-x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$-type positive electrode materials are used, the charge and discharge curves are sloping and a higher battery capacity can be achieved by charging to higher voltage, although this shortens battery life. The increased capacity is illustrated by the change in the specific energy or energy density of INCP 61/16/78 batteries charged to either 4.2 or 4.1 V as illustrated in Table 35.16 below. After charge to 4.2 V , the cells delivered 145 $\mathrm{Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $345 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$, whereas after charge to 4.1 V , the batteries delivered $131 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $325 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$. Similarly sized batteries fabricated using lithium manganese oxide spinel positive electrode material delivered $84 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $168 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$ after charge to 4.2 V .

TABLE 35.16 Specific Energy and Energy Density of Prismatic Li-ion Cells.
(Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)
| Cell | Voltage limits | Specific energy | Energy density |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| INCP 61/16/78 (7 Ah) | $4.2 \mathrm{~V}-2.5 \mathrm{~V}$ | $145 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ | $345 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$ |
| INCP 61/16/78 (6 Ah) | $4.1 \mathrm{~V}-2.5 \mathrm{~V}$ | $131 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ | $325 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$ |
| IMP 61/16/78 (4 Ah) | $4.2 \mathrm{~V}-2.5 \mathrm{~V}$ | $84 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ | $168 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$ |


Discharge Rate Capability of Wound Prismatic Batteries. The rate capability of a 1.4 Ah wound prismatic $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is illustrated by the discharge curves shown in Fig. 35.56. At the 0.2 C rate ( 270 mA ), the battery provided 1.4 Ah , at the C rate ( 1.35 A$), 1.36 \mathrm{Ah}$, and at the 2 C rate $(2.7 \mathrm{~A}), 0.8 \mathrm{Ah}$. The average voltage at the $\mathrm{C} / 5$ rate was $3.76 \mathrm{~V}, 3.5 \mathrm{~V}$ at the C rate, and 3.35 V at the 2 C rate.

Discharge of Wound Prismatic Batteries at High and Low Temperature. The discharge capability of wound prismatic batteries is indicated by the discharge curves shown in Fig. 35.57 for a 1.4 Ah wound prismatic design. When discharged at $0.945 \mathrm{~A}(0.7 \mathrm{C})$, at $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the battery delivered 1.4 Ah ; at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1.34 \mathrm{Ah}$; at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1.16 \mathrm{Ah}$; and at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, 0.85 Ah . The average voltage at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was 3.56 V ; at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 3.43 \mathrm{~V}$ and at $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 3.2 \mathrm{~V}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1104.jpg?height=523&width=952&top_left_y=680&top_left_x=461)
FIGURE 35.56 Performance of a 1.4 Ah wound prismatic CGP345010 $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery at various discharge rates to 3.0 V at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, after being charged at $945 \mu \mathrm{~A}$ to 4.2 V followed by a taper charge for 2 hours total at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1104.jpg?height=484&width=891&top_left_y=1410&top_left_x=491)
FIGURE 35.57 High and low temperature discharge of a 1.4 Ah CGP345010 $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ wound prismatic battery at 1350 mA . Charged at 945 mA to 4.2 V followed by taper charge for 2 hours total at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Panasonic.)

Storage Performance of Wound Prismatic Cells. The storage stability of wound prismatic $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries is indicated by the discharge curves shown in Fig. 35.58. As shown, after charge to 4.1 V then storage for 4 months at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the 0.63 mAh battery delivered 0.57 mAh , with a self discharge rate of $2.4 \% /$ month . After being recharged, the battery delivered 0.63 mAh , demonstrating that no irreversible capacity loss occurred on storage. The self-discharge performance of the battery is shown in Fig. 35.59. As shown, the selfdischarge rate is typically $2.4 \% /$ month and after 6 months storage at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 90 \%$ of the battery's capacity was delivered.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1105.jpg?height=526&width=982&top_left_y=599&top_left_x=431)
FIGURE 35.58 Discharge at 120 mA to 3.0 V of a wound prismatic $30486 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery before and after 4 month storage at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The battery was charged at 420 mA to 4.1 V on a CCCV regime followed by a taper charge for 2 hours total at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Panasonic.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1105.jpg?height=575&width=1014&top_left_y=1321&top_left_x=420)
FIGURE 35.59 Self-discharge performance of a $30486 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ battery at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Charging conditions: Constant voltage/constant current, $4.1 \mathrm{~V}, 420 \mathrm{~mA}$ (max.) 2 hours, $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Discharge conditions: Constant current 120 mA to 3.0 V at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Panasonic.)

Discharge of Flat-Plate Prismatic Spinel Batteries. Low temperature capability has been demonstrated in prismatic batteries that utilize a manganese-based spinel positive electrode material. Shown in Fig. 35.60 are discharge curves at $0.8 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{C} / 5)$ for a 4 Ah spinel cell at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. At $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the capacity delivered was $96 \%$ of that delivered at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ( 4.2 Ah ). At $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, 3.3 Ah was delivered, or $75 \%$ of the value at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. As is characteristic of the spinel chemistry, the voltage was higher than designs that employ $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ or Li $\mathrm{Ni}_{x} \mathrm{Co}_{1-x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$. At $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the average cell voltage was 3.85 V ; at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 3.43 \mathrm{~V}$; and at $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, 3.16 V.

Prismatic cells that utilize the spinel chemistry offer good cycle life at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ as illustrated in Fig. 35.61. Although spinel designs have fade rates typically twice that of cells that use cobalt-based chemistry, as illustrated, hundreds of cycles can be delivered without significant fade. However, spinel cells typically have more capacity fade at high temperature than those that use cobalt-based chemistry.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1106.jpg?height=621&width=1130&top_left_y=699&top_left_x=372)
FIGURE 35.60 Discharge curves for a 4 Ah prismatic spinel ( $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ ) battery when discharged at the 0.8 A rate at several temperatures.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1106.jpg?height=503&width=755&top_left_y=1451&top_left_x=558)
FIGURE 35.61 Capacity of a 4 Ah prismatic spinel cell when cycled at 0.8 A between 4.2 and 3.0 V at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

Pulse Discharge Performance of $6 \mathrm{Ah} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiNi}_{1-x} \mathrm{Co}_{x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ Flat-Plate Prismatic Cells. Many applications, including power tools, radios, thermal imaging devices, engine starters, and medical implants require high-rate pulse discharge capability, although the duty cycles vary greatly. To indicate the pulse discharge capability of a flat-plate prismatic $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiNi}_{1-x} \mathrm{Co}_{x} \mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{Li}$-ion battery, Fig. 35.62 shows the cell voltage during 0.2 second pulses at $50 \mathrm{~A}(8.3 \mathrm{C})$ to $125 \mathrm{~A}(20.8 \mathrm{C})$ on a 6 Ah cell. The positive electrode in these cells had a geometric area of $2106 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$. As shown, the minimum voltage at $50 \mathrm{~A}\left(24 \mathrm{~mA} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)$ was 2.81 V , at $75 \mathrm{~A}\left(36 \mathrm{~mA} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right) 2.45 \mathrm{~V}$, at $100 \mathrm{~A}\left(47 \mathrm{~mA} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right) 2.11 \mathrm{~V}$ and at $125 \mathrm{~A}(59 \mathrm{~mA} / \left.\mathrm{cm}^{2}\right), 1.76 \mathrm{~V}$. Thus at 125 A the cell delivered $1170 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $2933 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{L}$. A plot of the minimum voltage versus the discharge rate is shown in Fig. 35.63. As shown, for short, high-rate pulses, the minimum voltage is linearly related to the discharge rate. Figure 35.64 shows the voltage during a high-rate pulse discharge using a duty cycle of 25 A , 0.1 second pulses with 7.5 A continuous between pulses. In this regime, the cell delivered 5.28 Ah and the voltage drop during the pulse was typically less than 0.3 V , again illustrating the ability of this technology to provide high power.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1107.jpg?height=659&width=1024&top_left_y=903&top_left_x=413)
FIGURE 35.62 Pulse discharge of a 6 Ah flat-plate prismatic cell at 50 A to 125 A for 0.2 seconds at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Prior to the test the cell was aged for 36 weeks at room temperature then charged at 5 A. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1108.jpg?height=714&width=876&top_left_y=181&top_left_x=487)
FIGURE 35.63 Minimum cell voltage during 0.2 second pulse discharge of 6 Ah prismatic cell at various rates at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1108.jpg?height=701&width=904&top_left_y=1221&top_left_x=485)
FIGURE 35.64 Pulse discharge of a 6 Ah prismatic cell at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ using a duty cycle with 25 A, 0.1 second pulses and 7.5 A between cycles. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

Low-Temperature Discharge of Flat-Plate Prismatic 6 Ah Cells. Flat-plate prismatic cells can provide improved low temperature performance if they employ a ternary electrolyte specially formulated to broaden their temperature capability. Discharge curves at 1 A at temperatures from $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for a 6 Ah cell charged to 4.1 V are shown in Fig. 35.65. The capacity, energy and average voltage provided by the cell at temperatures from $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are shown in Table 35.17. At $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the product delivered 4.90 Ah and 16.8 Wh , or $82 \%$ of the capacity and $79 \%$ of the energy delivered at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and at $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the cell delivered 4 Ah and 11.6 Wh .

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1109.jpg?height=536&width=996&top_left_y=528&top_left_x=428)
FIGURE 35.65 Voltage of a 6 Ah prismatic cell charged to 4.1 V at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then discharged at 1 A at several temperatures. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

TABLE 35.17 Capacity and Energy of a 6 Ah Cell at Low Temperatures
| Temperature ( ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) | Capacity (Ah) | Energy (Wh) | $\mathrm{V}_{\text {avg }}(\mathrm{V})$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 25 | 5.97 | 21.35 | 3.57 |
| 0 | 5.15 | 18.34 | 3.56 |
| -20 | 4.90 | 16.78 | 3.42 |
| -30 | 4.64 | 14.92 | 3.22 |
| -40 | 4.06 | 11.57 | 2.85 |


Discharge Rate Capability of 7 Ah Flat-Plate Prismatic Cells. Flat-plate prismatic cells can provide higher rate capability than common wound products, as illustrated below for 7 Ah cells. This cell employs a different electrode configuration from the 6 Ah unit in the same case. The high rate capability is attributable to the cell design as flat-plate prismatic designs have many current collector tabs, versus the single or small number of tabs typical in wound units, minimizing ohmic polarization. These cells utilized the $\mathrm{LiNi}_{1-x} \mathrm{Co}_{x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$-type positive electrode materials and have a sloping charge curve, characteristic of Li-ion cells that use this material. As a result, cells can be charged to higher voltage, yielding higher capacity, although this typically adversely affects cell life. To illustrate the effect of charge voltage, data for cells charged to either 4.1 V or 4.2 V are compared.

Constant-current discharge curves for cells charged to either 4.1 V or 4.2 V at 1 A at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are shown in Fig. 35.66. At 0.36 A , the unit charged to 4.1 V delivered 6.8 Ah and 24.3 Wh, $10.5 \%$ less capacity and $12.2 \%$ less energy than the unit charged to 4.2 V. Both delivered high-rate capability; here discharge at rates up to 24 A ( 3.4 C ) are shown. For the cell charged to 4.2 V , the average voltage during the 24 A discharge was $3.24 \mathrm{~V}, 0.4 \mathrm{~V}$ less than that at $0.36 \mathrm{~A}(3.64 \mathrm{~V})$.

The performance characteristics of this 7 Ah prismatic cell as a function of discharge rate after charging to 4.2 V are summarized in Table 35.18.

At constant power, more significant differences in cell performance are observed between units charged to 4.1 V or 4.2 V . Constant power discharge curves for INCP 611678 cells are shown in Fig. 35.67; the energy delivered by these products is tabulated in Table 35.19 and Table 35.20. As illustrated, at rates up to the 1 E rate ( 22.5 W ), the batteries delivered energy comparable to that delivered at the lowest rate evaluated. For example, at 22.5 W , the unit charged to 4.2 V delivered $90 \%$ of the energy delivered at 1.35 W , and at 22.5 W the unit charged to 4.1 V delivered $86 \%$ of the energy delivered at 1.35 W . However, the unit charged to 4.2 V offered better performance at high rates. At 90 W ( 4 E ), the cell charged to 4.2 V delivered 21.9 Wh , or $78 \%$ of the value delivered at 1.35 W , whereas the cell charged to 4.1 V delivered 12.3 Wh at 90 W , or $50 \%$ of the value delivered at 1.35 W . This performance improvement for prismatic batteries charged to a higher voltage is achieved, however, at the expense of reduced cycle life. It must be emphasized that charging to higher voltages must be controlled to avoid lithium plating on the negative electrode, particularly at lower temperatures.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1110.jpg?height=1065&width=801&top_left_y=974&top_left_x=532)
FIGURE 35.66 Voltage vs. capacity for a 7 Ah INCP 61/16/78 cell when discharged at constant current at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ after charging to (a) 4.1 V or (b) 4.2 V at 1 A . (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

Cycle Life of Flat-Plate Prismatic Cells. Prismatic Li-ion cells offer high cycle life and can be cycled continuously at high rates. The capacity of INCP 60/61/78 cells cycled at the 1 A or 6 A rate for charge and discharge, with charge limited at either 4.2 V or 4.1 V and discharge limited to 3.0 V or 2.5 V is illustrated in Fig. 35.68. The average fade rate over the initial 300 cycles is listed in Table 35.21. As shown, the cells cycled at 1 A demonstrated better capacity retention than cells cycled at 6 A , and the cells cycled to 4.1 V demonstrated better capacity retention than those cycled to 4.2 V . However, in all cases the fade rate is low. For example, the cell cycled at 6 A ( 1 C rate) between 4.2 V and 3.0 V delivered 5.2 Ah on the 300th cycle, or 83\% of its initial capacity, and the cell cycled at 1 A between 4.1 V and 3.0 V delivered 5.8 Ah on the 300th cycle, or $90 \%$ of the initial value. To illustrate the cycle life of these cells over longer periods, Fig. 35.69 shows data from this test to 3000 cycles. As shown, after 300 cycles the fade rate of a cell cycled at 6 A was linear at $0.017 \% /$ cycle. As the cell was cycled, the average voltage decreased as resistance increased, as illustrated by the discharge curves in Fig. 35.70.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1113.jpg?height=508&width=1071&top_left_y=179&top_left_x=389)
FIGURE 35.68 Capacity of INCP $160 / 61 / 78$ cells when cycled at a constant current of 1 A and 6 A , for charge and discharge, between 4.2 or 4.1 V and 3.0 V or 2.5 V at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

TABLE 35.21 Voltage Limits and Fade Rate of INCP 160/61/78 Cells Cycled at 1 A or 6 A at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)
| Charge limit | Discharge limit | Rate | Fade rate cycles 1-300 | Fade rate cycles >300 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 4.1 V | 3.0 V | 1.0 A | 0.035\% / cycle | - |
| 4.2 V | 3.0 V | 1.0 A | 0.092\% / cycle | - |
| 4.1 V | 2.5 V | 6.0 A | 0.037\%/cycle | 0.017\% / cycle |
| 4.2 V | 3.0 V | 6.0 A | 0.056\% / cycle | 0.016\% / cycle |


![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1113.jpg?height=515&width=1095&top_left_y=1407&top_left_x=381)
FIGURE 35.69 Capacity of INCP 60/61/78 cells charged to 4.2 V or 4.1 V and discharged to 3.0 V or 2.5 V at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at 1 A or 6 A . The cells cycled at 1 A completed 300 cycles, whereas the cell cycled at 6 A completed 3000 cycles. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

Charged Stand Performance of 6 Ah Flat-Plate Prismatic Cell. The ability of a 6 Ah INCP 160/61/78 cell to provide discharge capability after prolonged charged stand is indicated by the data shown in Fig. 35.71. Prior to the test, the cell delivered 6.1 Ah. After a four month stand at full charge at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the cell provided 5.3 Ah , thus a self-discharge rate of $3.5 \%$ per month. After recharge, the cell provided 5.8 Ah , or $95 \%$ of the capacity provided four months earlier. When a similar test was performed on a cell stored at $50 \%$ DOD, $98 \%$ capacity recovery was observed after four months storage at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1114.jpg?height=616&width=1112&top_left_y=506&top_left_x=381)
FIGURE 35.70 Discharge curves for a 6 Ah prismatic Li-ion cell when cycled at 6 A between 4.1 V and 2.5 V at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1114.jpg?height=674&width=974&top_left_y=1282&top_left_x=450)
FIGURE 35.71 Discharge of a 6 Ah INCP 160/61/78 cell before and after 4month stand fully charged, and discharge after charge, all at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

Constant Current Discharge Rate Capability of $35 \mathrm{Ah} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiNi}_{1-x} \mathrm{Co}_{x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ Flat-Plate Prismatic Cells. The high rate, continuous discharge capability of a $35 \mathrm{Ah} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiNi}_{1-x} \mathrm{Co}_{x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ flat-plate prismatic cells at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is indicated by the discharge curves shown in Fig. 35.72 and the plot of discharge capacity versus rate in Fig. 35.73. These cells weigh 870 g and have outside dimensions of $9.45 \times 14.0 \times 2.74 \mathrm{~cm}$. At 50 A , they provide an average voltage of 3.5 V , at $125 \mathrm{~A}, 3.3 \mathrm{~V}$, at $175 \mathrm{~A}, 3.2 \mathrm{~V}$ and at $250 \mathrm{~A}, 3.0 \mathrm{~V}$. As illustrated in Fig. 35.73, on discharge at up to 50 A the cells offered over 32 Ah and at $125 \mathrm{~A}, 29 \mathrm{Ah}$. At 25 A , they provided $134 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $323 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$, and at $50 \mathrm{~A} 130 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and 313 $\mathrm{Wh} / \mathrm{L}$. At 250 A , the voltage plateaued at 3.35 V yielding a specific power and power density of $960 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{kg}$ and $2300 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{L}$.

Larger flat-plate prismatic cells of similar design offer comparable relative performance. See Fig. 35.76, which describes the capacity of a 35 Ah flat-plate prismatic battery at 0.1 C to C rates at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. At the C rate at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the capacity was $12 \%$ less than that at the $\mathrm{C} / 5$ rate.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1115.jpg?height=557&width=1004&top_left_y=732&top_left_x=424)
FIGURE 35.72 Discharge curves for a 35 Ah flat-plate prismatic cell at rates from 25 A to 250 A at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1115.jpg?height=560&width=776&top_left_y=1425&top_left_x=539)
FIGURE 35.73 Rate capability of a 35 Ah flat-plate prismatic cell at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

Pulse Discharge Performance of 35 Ah Prismatic Cells. Flat-plate prismatic cells can deliver high-rate pulse discharge at temperatures as low as $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The voltage of a 35 Ah prismatic cell during 10 second pulse discharge at $25 \mathrm{~A}, 50 \mathrm{~A}, 75 \mathrm{~A}$ and 100 A at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is shown in Fig. 35.74. At $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the voltage drop from the open circuit voltage is 0.2 V at 50 A and 0.36 V at 100 A . At $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the voltage drop from the open circuit voltage is 0.84 V at 50 A and 1.24 V at 100 A . The ability to deliver the 10 pulse at temperatures from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is illustrated in Fig. 35.75. As shown, the minimum cell voltage at 100 A at $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was 1.6 V .

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1116.jpg?height=478&width=1275&top_left_y=571&top_left_x=298)
FIGURE 35.74 Cell voltage during 10 second pulse discharge at $25 \mathrm{~A}, 50 \mathrm{~A}, 75 \mathrm{~A}$ and 100 A at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for a 35 Ah prismatic design.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1116.jpg?height=668&width=822&top_left_y=1284&top_left_x=526)
FIGURE 35.75 Minimum voltage during a 10 second pulse discharge of a 35 Ah prismatic cell at $25 \mathrm{~A}, 50 \mathrm{~A}, 75 \mathrm{~A}$ and 100 A .

Continuous Low Temperature Discharge of Flat-Plate Prismatic 35 Ah Cells. Figure 35.76 shows the discharge capacity of a 35 Ah prismatic cell at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. As shown, the cell provided rate capability to 1 C at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, delivering $80 \%$ of the capacity provided at the $\mathrm{C} / 5$ rate. From the 0.1 C to the 1 C rate, the capacity at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was typically $30 \%$ less than that at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

Prismatic 35 Ah cells that employ electrolytes specifically formulated for low temperatures have demonstrated charge and discharge capability at low temperature. Figure 35.77 shows the discharge of a 35 Ah cell at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ after charge at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. When discharged at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the battery delivered $24.5 \mathrm{Ah}, 72 \%$ of that provided at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ( 34.1 Ah ). After charge at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the cell provided 22 Ah when discharged at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 91 \%$ of that provided after charge at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, as shown in Fig. 35.78. After charge at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, discharge at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ provided 17.7 Ah , as shown in Fig. $35.79,81 \%$ of that delivered after charge at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and discharge at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(21.9 \mathrm{Ah})$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1117.jpg?height=449&width=908&top_left_y=682&top_left_x=472)
FIGURE 35.76 Rate capability of a 35 Ah flat-plate prismatic cell at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1117.jpg?height=634&width=882&top_left_y=1286&top_left_x=485)
FIGURE 35.77 Discharge of a Yardney 35 Ah prismatic Li-ion battery at 5 A at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ after charge at 5 A to 4.1 V at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL).)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1118.jpg?height=755&width=1065&top_left_y=177&top_left_x=402)
FIGURE 35.78 Discharge of a Yardney 35 Ah prismatic Li-ion cell at 5 A at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ after charge at 5 A to 4.1 V at either $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of JPL .)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1118.jpg?height=785&width=1084&top_left_y=1204&top_left_x=394)
FIGURE 35.79 Discharge of a Yardney 35 Ah prismatic Li-ion cell at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at 2.5 A after charge at 2.5 A at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of JPL.)

The 35 Ah cells also provide high rate capability at low temperatures, as indicated by discharge curves at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, shown in Fig. 35.80 , those at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, shown in Fig. 35.81 , and at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, shown in Fig. 35.82. When charged and discharged at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the discharge curves for rates from 2.5 A to 12.5 A are very similar, the delivered capacity ranged from 35.8 Ah to 34.4 Ah . The discharge voltages were also comparable. At $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the delivered capacity ranged from 25.2 Ah to 21.2 Ah for these rates. The average voltage at the 2.5 A rate, 3.5 V , was 0.22 V higher than that at $12.5 \mathrm{~A}, 3.27 \mathrm{~V}$. After charge at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, discharge at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at 2.5 A yielded 17.9 Ah , and 9.8 Ah at 12.5 A . The average voltage at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 2.5 A was $3.29 \mathrm{~V}, 0.06 \mathrm{~V}$ higher than at the 12.5 A rate at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}(3.23 \mathrm{~V})$. The capacity data is summarized in Fig. 35.83, which compares the discharge capacity on charge and discharge at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C},-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, at rates from 2.5 A to 12.5 A .

Storage Stability of 20 Ah Flat-Plate Prismatic Batteries. The ability of flat-plate prismatic batteries to retain capacity on storage is indicated by the data presented in Table 35.22 which lists capacity retention after storage at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at either $50 \%$ or $100 \%$ state of charge for 8 or 16 weeks. These cells were on the $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCo}_{x} \mathrm{Ni}_{1-x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ chemistry and used a ternary carbonate electrolyte. At or below $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the batteries retained over $97 \%$ of their capacity. At $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, storage at $100 \%$ DOD resulted in $81 \%$ capacity retention, while storage at a $50 \%$ state of charge resulted in $91 \%$ capacity retention.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1119.jpg?height=779&width=1076&top_left_y=1026&top_left_x=387)
FIGURE 35.80 Rate capability of a Yardney 35 Ah prismatic Li-ion cell when charged at 2.5 A to 4.1 V at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and discharged at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of JPL.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1120.jpg?height=768&width=1060&top_left_y=181&top_left_x=407)
FIGURE 35.81 Rate capability of 35 Ah prismatic Li-ion cells when charged at 2.5 A to 4.1 V at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and discharged at $-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of JPL.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1120.jpg?height=768&width=1060&top_left_y=1219&top_left_x=407)
FIGURE 35.82 Rate capability of 35 Ah prismatic Li-ion cells when charged at 2.5 A to 4.1 V at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and discharged at $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of JPL.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1121.jpg?height=578&width=1000&top_left_y=181&top_left_x=426)
FIGURE 35.83 Summary of the rate capability of Yardney 35 Ah prismatic Li-ion cells when charged and discharged at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C},-20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $-30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

TABLE 35.22 Storage Stability of 20 Ah Prismatic Li-ion Batteries. (Courtesy of JPL.)
| Storage temperature | State of charge | Storage time | Reversible capacity retention |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(32^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ | 50\% | 8 weeks | 98\% |
| $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(32^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ | 100\% | 8 weeks | 97\% |
| $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(104^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ | 50\% | 8 weeks | 99\% |
| $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(104^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ | 100\% | 8 weeks | 98\% |
| $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(122^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ | 100\% | 16 weeks | 81\% |
| $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\left(122^{\circ} \mathrm{F}\right)$ | 50\% | 16 weeks | 91\% |


### 35.5 CHARGE CHARACTERISTICS OF Li-ION BATTERIES

Li-ion cells are fabricated in the discharged state and thus must be charged before use. Liion cells are typically charged using either a constant current (CC) or constant current, constant voltage with a taper charge (CCCV) regime. This charge regime is accomplished through the use of battery management circuitry. At low rates ( $\mathrm{C} / 5$ ), the CC regime approximates the CCCV regime as the cell is fully charged when the upper voltage limit is attained.

Li-ion cells are typically charged to either 4.1 V or 4.2 V . While $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ is structurally stable at both potentials, ${ }^{91}$ the cutoff voltage affects cell performance when $\mathrm{LiNi}_{1-x} \mathrm{Co}_{x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ is used. ${ }^{92}$ When charged to 4.2 V , cells with $\mathrm{LiNi}_{1-x} \mathrm{Co}_{x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ positive electrode material can offer higher capacity, but with reduced cycle life and storage stability, relative to cells charged to 4.1 V. The capacity of C/LiNi ${ }_{1-x} \mathrm{Co}_{x} \mathrm{O}_{2}$ cells charged to 4.1 V or 4.2 V is illustrated in Fig. 35.66 for constant current discharge; delivered energy on constant power discharge is illustrated in Fig. 35.67. At low rates ( $\mathrm{C} / 10$ ), cells charged to 4.2 V delivered about $14 \%$ more capacity than those charged to 4.1 V , whereas at high rates ( 2 C ), typically $18 \%$ more capacity was delivered when the higher charge voltage was used. Although the cells charged to
4.2 V delivered more capacity, their capacity fade was higher. The capacity of the cells when cycled at constant current is illustrated in Fig. 35.68 and Fig. 35.69. As shown, even though the capacity fade rate of cells charged to 4.2 V was higher, up to 300 cycles they delivered greater capacity. Only when cycled for more than 500 cycles did cells charged to 4.1 V outperform cells charged to 4.2 V . Also illustrated by this data is the performance of Li-ion cells on high rate ( $6 \mathrm{~A}, \mathrm{C}$ rate) or low rate ( $1 \mathrm{~A}, \mathrm{C} / 6$ rate) charge. As shown, cells charged at the lower rate did offer higher capacity, although this difference was reduced after 300 cycles. Further, the capacity fade rates of the cells was comparable.

The voltage, percent charged and current profile for the CCCV charging of a C/LiMn ${ }_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ 18650 cell at 1.4 A with a voltage limit of 4.2 V , and a $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ cell at 1.65 A and 4.2 V, is shown in Fig. 35.84. Initially, as the cells are charged at constant current, the state of charge increases linearly while the cell voltage asymptotically approaches 4.2 V . In the constant voltage portion of the charge, the current decays as the cell approaches full charge. Li-ion cells have high coulombic efficiency, typically $99.9 \%$, and high energy efficiency, typically 95 to $98 \%$. Because of the absence of parasitic processes, such as the gas forming reactions common in aqueous systems, more complex charge methodologies, such as pulse charging, offer little additional benefit.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1122.jpg?height=1093&width=1168&top_left_y=868&top_left_x=353)
FIGURE 35.84 Voltage, state of charge and current for $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2} 18650$ cells in a 2.5 h CCCV charge profile with $1.4 \mathrm{~A}\left(\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}\right)$ or $1.65 \mathrm{~A}\left(\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}\right)$ maximum current and and 4.2 V maximum voltage. (Courtesy of NEC Moli Energy.)

Because Li-ion cells are degraded irreversibly by overcharge or overdischarge, and may vent if overcharged, Li-ion batteries typically employ battery management circuitry to ensure safe operation and prevent overcharge. This circuitry can also provide other features such as a state-of-charge gauge and temperature monitoring. Many manufacturers recommend charging at low rates (less than 0.1C) if the cell voltage is below 2.5 V .

Many applications require batteries to retain capacity on continuous float (constant voltage) charge. In this scenario, the cells are charged whenever their open circuit voltage is less than a predetermined threshold value. The ability of wound $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ cells to retain capacity on float charge is indicated in Fig. 35.85, which shows the capacity retained by cells float charged for up to 21 months. ${ }^{93}$ Capacity retention was temperature dependent and followed an Arrhenius relationship. The capacity loss rate increased by a factor of $\sim 1.3$ for each $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ rise in temperature.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1123.jpg?height=1484&width=910&top_left_y=628&top_left_x=470)
FIGURE 35.85 Capacity retention of wound ICP22846 C/LiCoO2 Li-ion cells float charged at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ or $45^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at $4.0 \mathrm{~V}, 4.05 \mathrm{~V}$ or 4.1 V . (Courtesy of Japan Storage Battery Co., Ltd.)

The ability of flat plate prismatic designs to retain capacity on "float" is indicated by the data shown in Fig. 35.86. After 12 weeks, discharge of the 5 Ah cells yielded 4.3 Ah , indicating $18 \%$ self-discharge had occurred. Subsequent cycling demonstrated that they retained over $98 \%$ of their reversible capacity.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1124.jpg?height=523&width=1260&top_left_y=452&top_left_x=307)
FIGURE 35.86 Float charge test of two different 5 Ah prismatic cells over 12 weeks at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Courtesy of Yardney Technical Products, Inc.)

### 35.6 SAFETY TESTING OF CYLINDRICAL C/LiCoO2 BATTERIES

Cylindrical 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries have been exposed to a variety of safety test regimes including UN, UL, IATA and military safety tests. These organizations, as well as the IEC, are currently developing standards for Li-ion cells and batteries that will address electrical testing, safety evaluation and transportation tests for Li-ion cells and batteries. The safety evaluation tests include electrical, mechanical and environmental tests. The results are summarized in Table 35.23.

Table 35.24 gives the results of typical safety tests performed on commercial 18650-type $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2} \mathrm{Li}$-ion batteries. On short circuit, performed by discharging through a minimum length of 16 AWG wire, the voltage dropped to zero as the temperature rose to a maximum of $69^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ after 130 minutes, after which the temperature returned to room temperature. Similar results are reported for nail penetration. When the battery was exposed to heat, up to $150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, leakage resulted. On crush, the cell voltage dropped to 0.8 V within 30 seconds as the temperature rose to $116^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ over 5 minutes after which the temperature returned to ambient as the voltage decayed to 0 V . Performance on impact was similar to crush, a maximum temperature of $117^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ was reached after 4 minutes after which the temperature decayed to ambient as the voltage decayed to 0 V . In both the crush and impact tests, leakage resulted. On forced discharge at 1.6 A , the battery voltage reached a minimum of -2 V after 8 minutes then recovered to -0.6 V , the maximum temperature on forced discharge was $58^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. In the overcharge test reported here, the cell was overcharged to a maximum of 12 V at 4.8 A (3C). In this test, the cell reached its maximum temperature of $56^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ two minutes after it was fully charged. After full charge was achieved, the voltage quickly reached the prescribed maximum of 12 V while the charging current went to zero for the remaining duration of the test ( 30 minutes). Note that in none of these tests did the cell spontaneously disassemble, and pressure was released through the vent mechanism when required.

TABLE 35.23 Summary of Safety Tests Included for Li-ion Batteries
| Type | Test | Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Electrical | Continuous charge <br> Short circuit <br> Forced discharge <br> Over charge <br> High rate charge | Charge at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ per manufacturers recommendation, hold at end of charge voltage for 28 days. <br> $<50 \mathrm{~m} \Omega$ to 0.1 V at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. <br> Discharge at 0.2 C , for 12.5 h . <br> Charge at manufacturer recommend rate to 2.5 times rated capacity <br> Charge at three times manufacturer's recommended rate. |
| Mechanical | Shock <br> Vibration <br> Crush <br> Free fall | 3 axis, minimum 75 g , peak 125 g to 175 g . <br> 0.8 mm amplitude, 10 Hz to 55 Hz <br> Between flat surfaces to 13 kN <br> 1 m to hard wood floor, 6 times |
| Environmental | High temp. storage <br> Thermal shock <br> Altitude <br> Exposure | Store at $75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 48 h <br> $75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for $48 \mathrm{~h}, 20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 30 h <br> 11.6 kPa for 6 h <br> Heat to $130^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, hold at $130^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 60 min . |


TABLE 35.24 Typical Safety Tests Performed on Commercial 18650 Li-ion Batteries. (Courtesy of Sanyo.)
| Test | Method | Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Short circuit | 16 AWG wire, $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ | No event, Tmax $=69^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ |
| Short circuit | 16 AWG wire, $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ | No event |
| Heat | Heat at $5^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min}$ to $150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, soak at $150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ for 10 min . | Leakage |
| Crush | Flat surface to 2500 psig ( 17.2 Mpa ). | Leakage, $\operatorname{Tmax}=116^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ |
| Impact | 20 lbs from 2 ft on a 5/16 in bar | Leakage, $\operatorname{Tmax}=117^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ |
| Humidity | $65^{\circ} \mathrm{C},>95 \%$ humidity | No event |
| Vibration | 0.03 in amplitude, 10 to 55 Hz | No event |
| Drop | 6 ft , for ten times onto concrete surface | No event |
| Forced discharge | $1.6 \mathrm{~A},-12 \mathrm{~V}$ minimum | No event, Tmax $=58^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ |
| Over charge | 12 V max, 4.8 A max | No event, Tmax $=56^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ |
| Fire exposure | Expose to fire | Fire, leakage, smoke |


### 35.7 POLYMER Li-ION BATTERIES

Polymer Li-ion batteries provide the performance characteristics of $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{ion}$ batteries, including their high specific energy and high energy density, in a thin, high aspect-ratio form factor. The technology addresses applications, in particular portable communications and computing devices which require a thin, large footprint rechargeable battery. While polymer Li-ion cells utilize the same active materials as cylindrical or prismatic Li -ion cells, in polymer $\mathrm{Li}-$ ion cells, flat, bonded electrodes are used to enable the fabrication of thin cells packaged within a barrier film, in contrast to the steel or aluminum cell case used in other Li-ion technologies. This construction is shown schematically in Fig. 35.87, which illustrates the various layers of the construction. A commercial product is shown in Fig. 35.88.

An attractive feature of $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ polymer Li -ion cells is their ability to sustain abuse. Safety and abuse tests passed by $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ polymer $\mathrm{Li}-$ ion cells are listed in Table 35.25. In addition, $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ polymer Li -ion cells can sustain nail penetration in the fully charged state or the overcharged state without explosion or fire.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1126.jpg?height=241&width=750&top_left_y=186&top_left_x=563)
FIGURE 35.87 Schematic diagram showing the construction of a polymer Li-ion cell. (Courtesy of Telecordia.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_0f58a1d4fa538b7e9853g-1126.jpg?height=547&width=824&top_left_y=625&top_left_x=526)
FIGURE 35.88 A 0.57 Ah polymer Li-ion battery. (Courtesy of Sanyo.)

TABLE 35.25 Safety and Abuse Tests Passed by $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ Polymer Li-ion Batteries
| Test | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| Flat crush | 13 kN between flat plates |
| External short, $23^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ | $<50 \mathrm{mOhm}, 23^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ |
| External short, $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ | $<50 \mathrm{mOhm}, 60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ |
| Forced discharge | Discharge to $-12 \mathrm{~V}, 250 \%$ of rated capacity |
| Shock | 75 G initial, 125 to 175 G peak |
| Vibration | $10-55-10 \mathrm{~Hz}, 90 \mathrm{~min}, 1.6 \mathrm{~mm}$ |
| Drop | Ten times, 6 ft onto concrete |
| Impact | 20 lbs from 2 ft onto a 8 mm insulated bar |
| Thermal cycling | $-40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, ten times |
| Altitude | Six hours at 11.6 kPa |
| Heating | $5^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{min}$ to $150^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, soak |
| Abnormal charge | Three times recommended rate to $250 \%$ of rated capacity |
| Humidity | Cycle $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $65^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 85 \%$ to $95 \%$ relative humidity |


The polymer Li-ion cells described here may be more accurately described as employing a gel electrolyte, as the electrolyte contains a monomeric, volatile liquid component absorbed into a polymeric host, in contrast to technologies which do not employ a volatile, liquid component, such as solid polymer electrolyte batteries. ${ }^{94}$ Because of the poor conductivity of currently available solid polymer electrolytes (solid polymer lithium batteries developed to date operate at $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to accommodate the low conductivity of the electrolyte) (see Sec. 34.4.2), current "polymer" Li-ion batteries incorporate less viscous, liquid components to improve the conductivity of the electrolyte, enabling their use at ambient temperatures.

The electrochemistry of polymer Li-ion cells covers a wide range of active materials and electrolyte compositions, comparable to those used in liquid-electrolyte Li-ion cells. Active materials include lithiated manganese oxides, such as $\mathrm{Li}_{1.05} \mathrm{Mn}_{1.95} \mathrm{O}_{4}, \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}, \mathrm{LiNiO}_{2}$ and its Co-doped derivatives, as positive (cathode) active materials, and graphitic and nongraphitic carbons capable of reversibly intercalating lithium as the negative (anode) active materials. Liquid electrolytes used in polymer Li-ion cells are comparable to those used in cylindrical or prismatic Li-ion cells, typically ca. 1 M solutions of $\mathrm{LiPF}_{6}$ in mixtures of ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), and other carbonate esters or other cosolvents.

### 35.7.1 Electrode and Battery Fabrication

The electrodes for polymer Li-ion cells may be cast from a viscous mixture (a slurry) composed of an active material (e.g., $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}, \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}, \mathrm{LiAl}_{0.05} \mathrm{Co}_{0.15} \mathrm{Ni}_{0.8} \mathrm{O}_{2}$, etc. for the positive electrode, and a microbead mesophase graphite, artificial graphite or milled graphite fiber for the negative electrode); a conductive additive (e.g., Super P carbon); a dissolved polymeric binder (e.g., PVDF-HFP, such as Kynar FLEX ${ }^{®}$ 2801); a medium-to-low-volatility plasticizer, such as dibutyl phthalate or propylene carbonate, and processing aids, including surfactants, antioxidants etc.; and a volatile solvent such as acetone or methyl ethyl ketone (MEK). A plastic separator film may be cast from a homogenized slurry of the same polymeric binder (PVDF-HFP), silanized fumed silica, or alumina, and a plasticizer, in a similar volatile solvent. ${ }^{95}$

In the Telcordia (formerly Bellcore) technology, an ancillary plasticizer such as dibutyl phthalate is incorporated into the resin that temporarily remains in the separator and/or electrode layers after their respective films are cast using a more volatile solvent. The plasticizer facilitates the densification of the electrodes under lower temperature and/or pressure than is required for the full densification of the electrode compositions typical in liquidelectrolyte Li-ion cells. This, in turn, prevents damage to the delicate expanded-metal current collector grids typically used in polymer Li-ion cells. The plasticizer also acts as a porosity modifier and preserver in the electrodes and the separator during processing. Further, the plasticizer facilitates formation of a bond between the electrodes and the separator. Often, identical or similar polymeric binders, typically based on commercially available poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoropropylene) (PVDF-HFP) copolymers, are used in both electrodes and the separator, facilitating the bonding of layers during the lamination step. The use of other polymers, copolymers and their mixtures, e.g., PVDF-CTFE (CTFE = chlorotrifluoroethylene), poly(vinyl chloride) (PVC) and polyacrylonitrile (PAN) has also been described. ${ }^{96}$

Electrode tapes are then densified and bonded to their corresponding expanded-metal current collector grids, aluminum in the case of the positive electrode and copper for the negative, using heated double-roll laminators or parallel-platen presses. The current collector grid can be either embossed into the outside surface of the electrode, or preferably, embedded between two layers of electrode tape. Finally, the electrodes are laminated to a plastic or microporous separator interposed between the electrodes.

The electrode-separator bonding can be achieved by lamination (calendering), again between a pair of preheated rollers or platens, followed by removal of the plasticizer. The plasticizer is removed by either supercritical or solvent extraction with an appropriate lowboiling solvent of the plasticizer, but non-solvent of the polymeric binder, such as propane, carbon dioxide, methanol, ether, or hexanes. Alternatively, the plasticizer is removed by simple evaporation, preferably under reduced pressure and at elevated temperature.

The de-plasticized (extracted or otherwise) cells are then packaged into vaporimpermeable, flexible, multilayer polymer-aluminum bags, dried under reduced pressure and elevated temperature to remove any adsorbed water, activated with a measured amount of liquid electrolyte solution, and sealed. The liquid electrolyte is rapidly absorbed into the microporous structure of the electrodes and separator, thus making the spillage of the liquid electrolyte from an open battery highly improbable.

A larger-capacity cell can contain one or more flat plates, each plate composed of one positive electrode and one negative electrode bonded to two opposite sides of an ionically conductive separator. The plates can be stacked as individual plates, Z-folded, or folded in other ways as permitted by the mechanical properties of the individual component layers. A popular option is a "bicell" configuration, where the central plate is a double-thickness electrode, typically a negative electrode, sharing a single current collector grid. Both faces of the central electrode are bonded via two separator layers to two slightly smaller counterelectrodes, typically positive electrodes, each having its separate current collector.

A prismatic battery of larger capacity can be assembled from one or more stacked bicells, whose current collector tabs are welded inside the packaging and thus present only a single metal foil feed-through tab connected to each of the two multi-plate electrodes. To facilitate multi-plate battery assembly and alignment, individual bicells may be Z-folded to prevent electrodes and current collectors from breaking at the fold line; they may also be connected by common current collector(s), separator(s), electrode layer(s), etc.

### 35.7.2 Energy Density of Polymer Li-ion Batteries

Current implementations of polymer Li-ion batteries technology achieve specific energy and energy density values that are somewhat higher than those reported for liquid-electrolyte Liion batteries. Since the electrochemistry as well as the volume and weight fractions of various components in these two technologies are similar, this gain may be attributed to the lighter, thinner packaging materials used in flat, bonded-electrode batteries, and possibly more efficient utilization of space.

The physical dimensions, specific energy and energy density of a selection of $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ polymer Li -ion batteries are listed in Table 35.26. Polymer Li-ion batteries offer a thin form factor, batteries with thickness from 1.2 mm to 8.4 mm are available, although 3.5 mm to 4.0 mm thick batteries are typical; available capacities range from 0.5 Ah to 8 Ah . The practical lower limit to polymer battery thickness is 0.5 mm . The batteries offer specific energy and energy density comparable to that delivered by cylindrical and prismatic Li-ion batteries, $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ batteries deliver 145 to $190 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$, and 270 to 400 $\mathrm{Wh} / \mathrm{L}$, whereas $\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ batteries deliver 130 to $144 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{kg}$ and 235 to $300 \mathrm{~Wh} / \mathrm{L}$.
